question: Define the Cartesian product of two sets A and B. Given the sets A = {1, 2} and B = {x, y}, list all elements of A × B. Then, explain the difference between a relation and the Cartesian product.
expectedPoints: Definition: A × B = {(a, b) | a ∈ A, b ∈ B}
Key Difference: A Cartesian product is the set of ALL possible ordered pairs. A relation is any subset of that Cartesian product.

question: Explain the properties of relations: reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric, and transitive. For each property, provide a small example relation on the set A = {1, 2, 3} that demonstrates the property and a counterexample that does not.
expectedPoints: Clear definitions for each of the four properties.
An example relation on A that satisfies each property (e.g., Reflexive: R = {(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)}).
A counterexample relation on A that violates each property (e.g., Not Reflexive: R = {(1,1), (2,2)} missing (3,3)).

question: Prove that the relation R on the set of integers Z, defined by R = {(a, b) | a ≡ b (mod 5)}, is an equivalence relation. Your proof must formally verify all three required properties (reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity) using the definition of congruence modulo 5.
expectedPoints: Proof of Reflexivity: Show for any a ∈ Z, a ≡ a (mod 5) because 5 divides (a - a) = 0.
Proof of Symmetry: Assume a ≡ b (mod 5), so 5|(a-b). Then (b-a) = -(a-b), so 5|(b-a), proving b ≡ a (mod 5).
Proof of Transitivity: Assume a ≡ b (mod 5) and b ≡ c (mod 5), so 5|(a-b) and 5|(b-c). Then (a-c) = (a-b)+(b-c), which is also divisible by 5, so a ≡ c (mod 5).
Conclusion: Since all three properties hold, R is an equivalence relation.
